Vifo vingi vinavyohusiana na tumbaku hutokea katika nchi zenye kipato cha chini na cha kati, ambazo mara nyingi hulengwa na kuingiliwa kwa sekta ya tumbaku na masoko. Tumbaku pia inaweza kuwa mbaya kwa wasiovuta sigara.
translations.reference: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Tobacco Summary:** Nicotine in tobacco is highly addictive, and tobacco use is a major risk factor for heart and vascular diseases, more than 20 different types of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targeted by tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be harmful to non-smokers.

translations.source: Uvutaji wa moshi wa sigara pia umehusishwa katika matokeo mabaya ya kiafya, na kusababisha vifo milioni 1.2 kila mwaka. Takriban nusu ya watoto wote huvuta hewa iliyochafuliwa na moshi wa tumbaku na watoto 65,000 hufa kila mwaka kutokana na magonjwa yanayohusiana na moshi wa sigara. Kuvuta sigara wakati wa ujauzito kunaweza kusababisha hali kadhaa za kiafya kwa watoto. Bidhaa za tumbaku zinazopashwa joto (HTPs) zina tumbaku na huwaweka watumiaji kwenye hewa chafu zenye sumu, nyingi ambazo husababisha saratani na ni hatari kwa afya. Mifumo ya kielektroniki ya utoaji wa nikotini (ENDS) na mifumo ya kielektroniki ya uwasilishaji isiyo ya nikotini (ENNDS), inayojulikana kama sigara za kielektroniki, haina tumbaku na inaweza au isiwe na nikotini, lakini inadhuru kwa afya na bila shaka si salama.
translations.reference: Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Cigarette smoke exposure is also linked to adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths each year. Approximately half of all children breathe air polluted with tobacco smoke, and 65,000 children die each year from diseases related to cigarette smoke. Smoking during pregnancy can lead to several health conditions in children. Tobacco heating products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to dirty, toxic air, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and certainly not safe."

translations.source: Hata hivyo, ni mapema mno kutoa jibu wazi kuhusu athari za muda mrefu za HTPs na/au matumizi ya sigara za kielektroniki. Athari: Inakadiriwa kuwa watu bilioni 1.3 duniani kote wanatumia bidhaa za tumbaku, 80% kati yao wakiwa katika nchi za kipato cha chini na cha kati. Utumiaji wa tumbaku huchangia umaskini kwa kubadilisha matumizi ya kaya kutoka kwa mahitaji ya kimsingi, kama vile chakula na malazi, hadi tumbaku. Tabia hii ya matumizi ni ngumu kudhibiti kwa sababu tumbaku ina uraibu sana. Pia husababisha vifo vya mapema na ulemavu wa watu wazima wenye umri wa kuzalisha katika kaya hivyo kusababisha kupungua kwa mapato ya kaya na kuongezeka kwa gharama za huduma za afya.
translations.reference: However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

However, it is too early to give a clear answer regarding the long-term effects of Heated Tobacco Products (HTPs) and/or the use of electronic cigarettes. Impact: It is estimated that 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This pattern of consumption is difficult to control because tobacco is highly addictive. It also causes premature deaths and disability among working-age adults in households, leading to a decrease in household income and an increase in healthcare costs.

translations.source: Pamoja na athari mbaya za tumbaku kwa afya, jumla ya gharama za kiuchumi za uvutaji wa sigara (kutokana na matumizi ya afya na upotevu wa tija kwa pamoja) inakadiriwa kuwa karibu dola za Marekani trilioni 1.4 kwa mwaka, sawa na asilimia 1.8 ya pato la taifa kwa mwaka (GDP). Takriban 40% ya gharama hii ilitokea katika nchi zinazoendelea, ikionyesha mzigo mkubwa ambao nchi hizi zinakabiliwa. Ushuru wa tumbaku hutumiwa kwa kiasi ili kukabiliana na athari hii na inaonekana kama njia ya gharama nafuu zaidi ya kuzuia matumizi ya tumbaku, hasa miongoni mwa vijana na watu wa kipato cha chini. Ongezeko la ushuru ambalo linaongeza bei ya tumbaku kwa 10% hupunguza matumizi ya tumbaku kwa takriban 4% katika nchi zenye mapato ya juu na karibu 5% katika nchi zenye kipato cha chini na cha kati. Jibu la WHO: Kiwango cha janga hili la kibinadamu na kiuchumi ni la kushangaza, lakini pia linaweza kuzuilika.
translations.reference: In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable.
translations.hypothesis: Despite the harmful effects of tobacco on health, the total economic costs of smoking (from healthcare use and lost productivity combined) are estimated to be nearly US$1.4 trillion per year, equivalent to 1.8% of national income per year (GDP). Approximately 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the significant burden these countries face. Tobacco taxes are used in part to address this impact and are seen as a cost-effective way to prevent tobacco use, especially among young people and those with low incomes. A tax increase that raises the price of tobacco by 10% reduces tobacco use by approximately 4% in high-income countries and nearly 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO’s response: The scale of this humanitarian and economic crisis is staggering, but it is also preventable.

translations.source: Tumbaku Kubwa - pamoja na watengenezaji wote wa bidhaa za tumbaku - inapigania kuhakikisha hatari za bidhaa zao zinafichwa, lakini tunapambana: Mnamo 2003, Nchi Wanachama wa WHO zilipitisha kwa kauli moja Mkataba wa Mfumo wa WHO wa Kudhibiti Tumbaku (WHO FCTC), mkataba pekee wa afya ya umma chini ya udhamini wa WHO. Imetumika tangu 2005, kwa sasa ina Vyama 181. Ili kusaidia nchi kutekeleza WHO FCTC, WHO ilianzisha MPOWER, kifurushi cha hatua za kiufundi na rasilimali, ambayo kila moja inalingana na angalau kifungu kimoja cha WHO FCTC. MPOWER inajenga uwezo wa nchi kutekeleza hatua 6 za kupunguza mahitaji ya bidhaa za tumbaku: kufuatilia matumizi ya tumbaku na sera za kuzuia, kulinda watu dhidi ya matumizi ya tumbaku, kutoa msaada wa kuacha matumizi ya tumbaku, kuonya juu ya hatari ya tumbaku, kutekeleza marufuku ya utangazaji wa tumbaku kukuza na kufadhili na kuongeza kodi kwenye tumbaku.
translations.reference: Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

Big Tobacco – along with all tobacco product manufacturers – fights to ensure the dangers of their products are hidden, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. It has been in force since 2005 and currently has 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO launched MPOWER, a package of technical actions and resources, each corresponding to at least one article of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds capacity for countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitoring tobacco use and prevention policies, protecting people from tobacco use, providing support to quit tobacco use, warning about the dangers of tobacco, implementing bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship, and increasing taxes on tobacco.

translations.source: Trakoma Muhtasari: Trakoma ni ugonjwa wa macho na unaongoza kwa kusababisha upofu duniani kote. Inasababishwa na bakteria ya lazima ndani ya seli inayoitwa Chlamydia trachomatis. Maambukizi hupitishwa kutoka kwa mtu hadi kwa mtu kwa uhamisho wa moja kwa moja au usio wa moja kwa moja wa kutokwa kwa macho na pua ya watu walioambukizwa; uhamisho usio wa moja kwa moja unajumuisha kubeba kwenye mwili wa aina za nzi. Watoto wa umri wa shule ya mapema huhifadhi hifadhi kuu ya maambukizi.
translations.reference: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Trachoma Summary:** Trachoma is an eye disease and is a leading cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis, which thrives inside cells. Infection is transmitted from person to person through direct or indirect contact with the eye and nasal discharge of infected people; indirect transmission includes carrying it on the bodies of certain fly species. Children of early school age hold the main reservoir of infection.

translations.source: Mifano zinaeleza kuwa mtu anahitaji zaidi ya maambukizi 150 kwa maisha yote ili kuendeleza matatizo ya upofu ya trakoma. Trakoma imeenea katika baadhi ya watu maskini zaidi duniani, ambao wanaishi vijijini na maeneo ya mbali na hawana upatikanaji wa kutosha wa maji, usafi wa mazingira na huduma za afya. Afrika ndilo bara lililoathiriwa zaidi, lakini pia lina juhudi kubwa zaidi za kudhibiti. Ugonjwa huo pia hupatikana Amerika ya Kati na Kusini, Asia, Australia, na Mashariki ya Kati. Kufikia 2 Januari 2020, nchi 13 zilikuwa zimeripoti kufikia malengo ya kuondoa.
translations.reference: Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

"Examples show that a person needs more than 150 infections throughout their lifetime to develop the blinding disease trachoma. Trachoma is widespread among some of the poorest people in the world, who live in rural and remote areas and have insufficient access to water, sanitation, and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the greatest efforts to control it. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of January 2, 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals."

translations.source: Katika mwaka wa 2019 pekee, watu 92 622 walipata matibabu ya upasuaji kwa trakoma ya hali ya juu na milioni 95.2 walitibiwa kwa viua vijasumu. Licha ya mafanikio hayo, ugonjwa huu bado ni tatizo kubwa la afya ya jamii, huku kukiwa na wastani wa hasara ya dola za Marekani bilioni 8 kwa mwaka katika uzalishaji kutokana na upofu na ulemavu wa macho. Dalili: Maambukizi yanahusishwa na mabadiliko ya uchochezi katika kiwambo cha sikio inayojulikana kama "trakoma hai". Kinga ya mtu binafsi inaweza kufuta sehemu moja ya maambukizi, lakini katika jumuiya za endemic, kupatikana tena kwa viumbe hutokea mara kwa mara. Baada ya miaka ya matukio ya mara kwa mara ya trakoma hai, ndani ya kope inaweza kuwa na kovu kali sana (kovu la trachomatous conjunctival scarring) kwamba hugeuka ndani na kusababisha kope kusugua kwenye mboni ya jicho (trachomatous trichiasis), na kusababisha maumivu ya mara kwa mara na kutovumilia mwanga; mabadiliko haya na mengine ya jicho yanaweza kusababisha kovu ya konea.
translations.reference: In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

In 2019 alone, 92,622 people received surgical treatment for severe trachoma, and 9.52 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, this disease remains a major public health problem, with an average loss of US$8 billion per year in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: The infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the inner ear known as "active trachoma." Individual immunity may clear one episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-infection of the organism occurs frequently. After years of recurrent active trachoma, the inner eyelid can develop severe scarring (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that turns inward and causes the eyelid to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), causing repeated pain and light intolerance; these and other changes in the eye can lead to entropion scarring.

translations.source: Ikiachwa bila kutibiwa, hali hii husababisha kuundwa kwa opacities isiyoweza kutenduliwa, na kusababisha uharibifu wa kuona au upofu. Umri ambao hii hutokea inategemea mambo kadhaa ikiwa ni pamoja na ukubwa wa maambukizi ya ndani. Katika jamii zenye magonjwa mengi, inaweza kutokea utotoni, ingawa mwanzo wa ulemavu wa kuona kati ya umri wa miaka 30 na 40 ni kawaida zaidi. Wanawake hupofushwa hadi mara nne zaidi ya wanaume, labda kutokana na kuwasiliana mara kwa mara na watoto walioambukizwa. Watu wanaoishi katika jamii maskini zaidi na za mbali zaidi za Afrika, Asia, Australia, Mashariki ya Kati, Amerika ya Kati na Kusini na Visiwa vya Pasifiki wako hatarini.
translations.reference: Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"If left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, resulting in visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors, including the size of the internal infection. In communities with high disease prevalence, it can occur in childhood, although the onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 is more common. Women are affected up to four times more often than men, possibly due to frequent contact with infected children. People living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America, and the Pacific Islands are at risk."

translations.source: Matibabu: Trakoma inaweza kuzuiwa kwa kupunguza mambo hatarishi ya kimazingira. Haya ni pamoja na ukosefu wa usafi wa mazingira, kaya zenye msongamano wa watu, ukosefu wa maji na uhaba wa upatikanaji na matumizi ya vyoo. Maambukizi kwa kawaida huanza katika umri mdogo na kukua chini ya mara kwa mara kwa muda mfupi na kuongezeka kwa umri. Maambukizi mara nyingi hupatikana kwa njia ya familia na kuwasiliana na wengine wenye ugonjwa hai. Maambukizi ya kujirudia husababisha dalili mbaya zaidi za ugonjwa huo.
translations.reference: Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by reducing risky environmental factors. These include lack of environmental sanitation, overcrowded households, lack of water, and limited access and use of toilets. Infection typically begins at a young age and progresses slowly for a short period, increasing with age. Infection is often spread through families and contact with others who have the disease. Recurrent infections cause more severe symptoms of the disease.

translations.source: Trakoma inaweza kuondolewa kama tatizo la afya ya umma kwa kutumia mkakati SALAMA, unaojumuisha: Upasuaji wa kutibu trichiasis ya trachomatous, hatua ya upofu ya ugonjwa huo; Dawa za antibiotiki za kuondoa maambukizi, hasa usimamizi wa dawa nyingi za antiniotiki azithromycin, ambayo hutolewa na mtengenezaji kwa programu za kitaifa kupitia Mpango wa Kimataifa wa Trakoma; usafi wa uso; na Uboreshaji wa mazingira, hasa upatikanaji bora wa maji na usafi wa mazingira.
translations.reference: Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem by using the SALAMA strategy, which includes: surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; antibiotic medications to eliminate infection, particularly the mass administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is provided by the manufacturer to national programs through the International Trachoma Program; facial hygiene; and environmental improvement, especially better access to water and sanitation.

translations.source: WHO ilipitisha mkakati wa SALAMA mnamo 1993 na inaendelea katika jukumu lake la kutoa uongozi na uratibu wa juhudi za kimataifa za kuondoa trakoma kama shida ya afya ya umma.
translations.reference: WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO adopted the SALAMA strategy in 1993 and continues in its role of providing leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.

translations.source: Dawa ya asili, Nyongeza na Shirikishi Muhtasari: Dawa ya jadi Dawa ya jadi ina historia ndefu. Ni jumla ya maarifa, ustadi, na mazoea kulingana na nadharia, imani, na uzoefu wa asili wa tamaduni tofauti, iwe ya kuelezeka au la, inayotumika katika kudumisha afya na vile vile katika kuzuia, utambuzi, uboreshaji au matibabu ya magonjwa ya kimwili na kiakili. Dawa ya ziada
translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

**Natural Medicine, Supplement and Comprehensive Summary: Traditional Medicine**

Traditional medicine has a long history. It is a sum of knowledge, skills, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and natural experiences of different cultures, whether explainable or not, used in maintaining health as well as in preventing, diagnosing, improving, or treating physical and mental illnesses. Supplementary Medicine.

translations.source: Maneno "dawa ya ziada" au "dawa mbadala" yanarejelea seti pana ya mazoea ya utunzaji wa afya ambayo si sehemu ya mila ya nchi hiyo au tiba ya kawaida na haijajumuishwa kikamilifu katika mfumo mkuu wa huduma ya afya. Zinatumika kwa kubadilishana na dawa za jadi katika nchi zingine. Dawa za mitishamba Dawa za mitishamba ni pamoja na mimea, mimea, maandalizi ya mitishamba na bidhaa za mitishamba zilizomalizika, ambazo zina viungo hai vya sehemu za mimea, au vitu vingine vya mimea, au mchanganyiko. Athari: Mkakati wa Tiba Asilia wa WHO 2014–2023 ulitayarishwa na kuzinduliwa kwa kuitikia azimio la Bunge la Afya Ulimwenguni kuhusu tiba asilia (WHA62.13).
translations.reference: The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

"The terms "complementary medicine" or "alternative medicine" refer to a broad range of health care practices that are not part of the country's traditional medical practices or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the mainstream health care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicines in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include plants, plant materials, herbal preparations, and finished herbal products, which contain active ingredients from plant parts, or other plant substances, or combinations. Action: The WHO’s Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13)."

translations.source: Mkakati huo unalenga kusaidia Nchi Wanachama katika kuunda sera tendaji na kutekeleza mipango ya utekelezaji ambayo itaimarisha jukumu la tiba asilia katika kuweka idadi ya watu kuwa na afya bora. Kushughulikia changamoto, kukabiliana na mahitaji yaliyoainishwa na Nchi Wanachama na kuendeleza kazi iliyofanywa chini ya mkakati wa tiba asilia wa WHO: 2002-2005, mkakati uliosasishwa wa kipindi cha 2014-2023 unatoa kipaumbele zaidi kuliko mtangulizi wake katika kuweka kipaumbele huduma za afya na mifumo, ikiwa ni pamoja na bidhaa za tiba asilia na nyongeza, mazoea na watendaji. Malengo ya kimkakati ni: Kujenga msingi wa maarifa kwa usimamizi hai wa T&CM kupitia sera zinazofaa za kitaifa; Kuimarisha uhakikisho wa ubora, usalama, matumizi sahihi na ufanisi wa T&CM kwa kudhibiti bidhaa, mazoea na watendaji;
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili document:

"This strategy aims to help Member States in developing effective policies and implementing implementation plans that will strengthen the role of traditional medicine in keeping the population healthy. To address challenges, respond to the needs identified by Member States, and build on the work done under the WHO’s traditional medicine strategy 2002-2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014-2023 gives greater priority than its predecessor to health services and systems, including traditional medicine products and supplements, practices, and practitioners. The strategic goals are: To build a knowledge base for sound management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen quality assurance, safety, proper use, and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices, and practitioners."



**Explanation of some key terms:**

*   **T&CM:** Traditional and Complementary Medicine (This is a common abbreviation used in this context)

translations.source: Kukuza huduma ya afya kwa wote kwa kuunganisha huduma za T&CM katika utoaji wa huduma za afya na kujihudumia. Jibu la WHO: Ripoti ya kimataifa ya WHO kuhusu tiba asilia na nyongeza ya 2019 ilitengenezwa ili kushughulikia pengo la data za kuaminika, zinazoaminika na rasmi kutoka kwa Nchi Wanachama katika eneo la T&CM. Ripoti hii hukagua maendeleo ya kimataifa katika T&CM katika kipindi cha miongo miwili iliyopita na inategemea michango kutoka Nchi 179 Wanachama wa WHO. Inatoa taarifa muhimu kwa watunga sera, wataalamu wa afya na umma kwa kutumia mchango unaowezekana wa T&CM kwa afya na ustawi.
translations.reference: To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

"Promoting health services for all by integrating Traditional and Complementary Medicine (T&CM) services into health service delivery and self-care. WHO's Response: The WHO’s international report on traditional medicine and a 2019 addendum were developed to address the gap in reliable, credible, and formal data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews international developments in T&CM over the past two decades and draws on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides essential information for policymakers, health professionals, and the public regarding the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being."

translations.source: Kupandikiza Muhtasari: Kupandikiza seli za binadamu, tishu na viungo Kupandikiza seli za binadamu, tishu au viungo huokoa maisha ya watu wengi na kurejesha kazi muhimu ambapo hakuna njia mbadala za ufanisi kulinganishwa zilizopo. Katika miaka 50, upandikizaji umekuwa jambo la mafanikio duniani kote. Hata hivyo, kuna tofauti kubwa kati ya nchi katika upatikanaji wa upandikizaji unaofaa na katika kiwango cha usalama, ubora, ufanisi wa mchango na upandikizaji wa seli za binadamu, tishu na viungo.
translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Transplanting Summary:** Transplanting human cells, tissues, and organs Transplanting human cells, tissues, or organs saves the lives of many people and restores essential functions where no other effective alternatives exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a success worldwide. However, there is a significant difference between countries in the availability of appropriate transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficiency of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs.

translations.source: Vipengele vya maadili vya upandikizaji viko mbele. Hasa, mahitaji ya wagonjwa ambayo hayajafikiwa na uhaba wa vipandikizi husababisha jaribu la usafirishaji wa viungo vya mwili wa binadamu kwa ajili ya upandikizaji. Kupandikiza binadamu Seli za binadamu na tishu za kupandikiza zinaweza kuokoa maisha au kurejesha kazi muhimu. Kwa mfano pandikizi la corneal linaweza kurejesha kuona kwa upofu wa corneal;
translations.reference: The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;

"Ethical considerations of transplantation are at the forefront. Specifically, the needs of patients that are not met due to a shortage of organs lead to the trial of human body parts transportation for transplantation. Human transplantation of human cells and transplant tissues can save lives or restore essential functions. For example, a corneal transplant can restore sight to those with corneal blindness."

translations.source: upandikizaji wa seli za shina za hematopoietic unaweza kuponya magonjwa ya kuzaliwa au kupatikana ikiwa ni pamoja na baadhi ya leukemia. Tazama habari zaidi juu ya Seli za Shina za Hematopoeitic upandikizaji wa vali ya moyo wa binadamu mara nyingi hujumuisha hali bora ya uingizwaji na wagonjwa wanaopokea hawahitaji tiba ya muda mrefu ya kuzuia mgando. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, mnyama kwa binadamu, inayofafanuliwa kama chembe hai, tishu au viungo vya asili ya wanyama na maji maji ya mwili wa binadamu, seli, tishu au viungo ambavyo vina ex vivo na nyenzo hizi hai, xenogeneic, ina uwezo wa kuunda nyenzo mbadala ya asili ya mwanadamu na kuziba upungufu katika nyenzo za binadamu kwa ajili ya upandikizaji.
translations.reference: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

"Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can cure congenital or acquired diseases, including some leukemias. See more information about Hematopoietic Stem Cell transplantation. Human heart valve transplantation often involves excellent implantation conditions and patients receiving it do not require long-term anticoagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation, defined as animal-to-human, living cells, tissues, or organs of animal origin into the human body, cells, tissues, or organs that have been ex vivo and these living, xenogeneic materials, have the potential to create a human-like substitute material and fill the shortage of human materials for transplantation."

translations.source: Uchangiaji: Michango kutoka kwa watu walioaga dunia inapaswa kuendelezwa kwa uwezo wao wa juu zaidi wa kimatibabu ili kuepuka, inapowezekana, hatari za asili kwa wafadhili hai, kama ilivyoelezwa katika Kanuni ya 3 ya Mwongozo wa WHO. Kuna hitaji linalotambulika kwa jamii, na wataalamu wa afya, kuelimishwa vyema kuhusu uchangiaji na upandikizaji na huo ndio ufunguo wa mafanikio ya mipango ya uchangiaji wa marehemu. Hata hivyo, licha ya matumizi ya mara kwa mara ya vifaa vinavyotolewa na wafadhili waliofariki, michango kutoka kwa wafadhili wanaoishi ni muhimu kwa baadhi ya aina za upandikizaji au kufidia ugavi mdogo wa nyenzo zinazopatikana kutoka kwa wafadhili waliofariki ili kukidhi mahitaji ya mgonjwa. Utoaji hai unatekelezwa licha ya ukweli kwamba unahusisha hatari kwa wafadhili ambazo haziwezi kupuuzwa. Kwa vile ununuzi wa nyenzo za binadamu kwa ajili ya kupandikiza kutoka kwa wafadhili waliokufa au wanaoishi na upandikizaji wa alojeneki unaofuata unaweza kuhusisha hatari za kimaadili na kiusalama kwa mpokeaji na mfadhili, udhibiti mkali na uangalizi unaofaa unafaa kutekelezwa na mamlaka za afya ili kuwalinda.
translations.reference: Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Contribution:** Contributions from deceased individuals should be developed to the highest possible medical capacity in order to avoid, where possible, natural risks to living donors, as outlined in Rule 3 of the WHO Guidance. There is a recognized need for the community and healthcare professionals to be well-educated about donation and transplantation, and this is the key to the success of deceased donor contribution programs. However, despite the frequent use of materials provided by deceased donors, contributions from living donors are essential for some types of transplantation or to compensate for the limited supply of materials available from deceased donors to meet patient needs. Living donation is carried out despite the fact that it involves risks to donors that cannot be ignored. Because the procurement of human materials for transplantation from deceased or living donors and subsequent transplant of the deceased can involve ethical and safety risks for the recipient and donor, strict regulation and appropriate oversight are appropriate to be implemented by health authorities to protect them.

translations.source: Kanuni Elekezi huamuru utunzaji bora kwa wafadhili na wapokeaji. Uangalizi wa uwazi wa mamlaka za afya juu ya shughuli za mchango na upandikizaji pia ni muhimu ili kuongeza imani ya umma katika mfumo. Kwa kuongezea, uamuzi wa kuwa mfadhili mara nyingi hutegemea ufahamu kwamba mchango katika upatikanaji wa rasilimali za upandikizaji unaweza kufaidisha mahitaji ya afya ya familia ya mfadhili siku moja. Ikiwa tumejiandaa kupokea upandikizaji tukihitaji, basi tunapaswa kuwa tayari kutoa Maadili: Kanuni Mwongozo wa WHO kuhusu Seli ya Binadamu, Tishu na Upandikizaji wa Kiungo
translations.reference: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation

Transparent oversight of health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also crucial to increase public trust in the system. Furthermore, the decision to become a donor often relies on the understanding that contributing to the availability of transplantation resources may benefit the donor's family's health needs one day. If we prepare ourselves to receive a transplant should we need it, then we should be ready to give. Ethics: Guiding Principles of WHO on Human Cells, Tissues and Organ Transplantation.

translations.source: Mnamo 1987 Mkutano wa Arobaini wa Afya Ulimwenguni, unaohusika na biashara ya faida katika viungo vya binadamu, ulianzisha utayarishaji wa Kanuni za Kwanza za Mwongozo wa WHO juu ya Uhamishaji, iliyoidhinishwa na Bunge mnamo 1991 katika azimio la WHA44.25. Kanuni hizi Elekezi zimeathiri pakubwa kanuni na taratibu za kitaaluma pamoja na sheria kote ulimwenguni takriban miongo miwili. Baada ya mchakato wa mashauriano uliochukua miaka kadhaa, Bunge la Sitini na tatu la Afya Duniani lilipitisha azimio WHA63.22 tarehe 21 Mei 2010, likiidhinisha Kanuni za Mwongozo wa WHO zilizosasishwa na kubainisha maeneo ya maendeleo ili kuboresha uchangiaji na upandikizaji. Azimio la Istanbul Wataalamu wa mchango na upandikizaji kutoka mikoa yote, kupitia mashirika na taasisi zao nyingi, wanaidhinisha Azimio la Istanbul kuhusu usafirishaji haramu wa viungo na utalii wa kupandikiza ulioendelezwa chini ya uongozi wa Jumuiya ya Upandikizaji na Jumuiya ya Kimataifa ya Nephrology mnamo Mei 2008.
translations.reference: In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"In 1987, the Fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned with profit-making business in human organs, initiated the drafting of the First Set of WHO Guiding Principles on Transnational Organ Procurement, approved by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have significantly influenced professional standards and procedures as well as laws worldwide for nearly two decades. Following a consultation process lasting several years, the Sixty-Third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, approving the updated WHO Guiding Principles and specifying areas for development to improve donation and transplantation. Istanbul Declaration Transplant professionals from all regions, through their numerous organizations and institutions, endorse the Istanbul Declaration on the illicit trafficking of organs and transplant tourism, developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008."

translations.source: Usafiri na afya Muhtasari: Usafiri wa kimataifa unaweza kusababisha hatari mbalimbali kwa afya, kulingana na sifa za msafiri na wasafiri. Wasafiri wanaweza kukutana na mabadiliko ya ghafla na makubwa katika urefu, unyevu, vijidudu, na joto, ambayo inaweza kusababisha afya mbaya. Aidha, hatari kubwa za kiafya zinaweza kutokea katika maeneo ambayo malazi hayana ubora, usafi na usafi wa mazingira hautoshi, huduma za matibabu hazijaendelezwa vizuri na maji safi hayapatikani. Usafiri wa kimataifa unaongezeka, kwani mamilioni ya watu husafiri kwa madhumuni ya kitaaluma, kijamii, burudani na kibinadamu kila mwaka.
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Travel and Health Summary:** International travel can lead to various health risks, depending on the characteristics of the traveler and the destinations. Travelers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, germs, and temperature, which can lead to illness. Furthermore, serious health risks can occur in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, environmental hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed, and clean water is unavailable. International travel is increasing, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational, and humanitarian purposes every year.

translations.source: Wasafiri wote lazima wajitayarishe kwa aina mbalimbali za hatari za kiafya wanazoweza kukabiliwa nazo katika mazingira yasiyofahamika kabla, wakati na baada ya kusafiri. Watu wanaopanga kusafiri wanapaswa kutafuta ushauri kuhusu hatari zinazoweza kutokea katika maeneo waliyochagua na kuelewa jinsi bora ya kulinda afya zao na kupunguza hatari ya kupata magonjwa. Kupanga mbele, hatua zinazofaa za kuzuia na tahadhari makini zinaweza kulinda afya ya wasafiri, na kupunguza hatari za ajali na kupata magonjwa. Ingawa taaluma ya matibabu na sekta ya usafiri inaweza kutoa usaidizi wa kina na ushauri mzuri, inasalia kuwa jukumu la msafiri kutafuta maelezo, kuelewa hatari zinazohusika na kuchukua tahadhari zinazohitajika ili kulinda afya zao wakati wa kusafiri. Hatari: Wakati wa kubainisha hatari za kiafya za usafiri wa kimataifa, wasafiri na wataalamu wa afya wanapaswa kuzingatia mambo muhimu yafuatayo:
translations.reference: All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors:

**Traveler's Habits and Basic Traveler's Health**

Travelers may encounter the following health risks depending on their destination: bites from animals and insects including mosquitoes, ticks, mites, and certain flies that can spread diseases such as malaria, those originating from blood or other bodily fluids.

translations.source: mabadiliko ya mazingira, kama vile urefu, unyevu, joto magonjwa yatokanayo na chakula na maji huduma duni za matibabu majeraha, ikiwa ni pamoja na ajali za barabarani au shughuli za burudani afya ya kisaikolojia, pamoja na mafadhaiko na mshtuko wa kitamaduni
translations.reference: environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili text:

"Environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, heat, food and waterborne illnesses, poor medical care, injuries, including traffic accidents or recreational activities, psychological health, including stress and cultural shock."

translations.source: Wasafiri wanaweza pia kukabiliwa na magonjwa kadhaa ya kuambukiza, na mengi ya haya yanaweza kuzuiwa kwa chanjo. Hata hivyo, kuna baadhi ya magonjwa ya kuambukiza, ikiwa ni pamoja na baadhi ya hatari zaidi, ambayo hakuna chanjo zilizopo. Maeneo ambayo malazi, usafi na usafi wa mazingira, huduma ya matibabu na ubora wa maji ni wa kiwango cha juu husababisha hatari chache kwa afya ya wasafiri. Pale ambapo huduma hizi hazitolewi, wasafiri wanapaswa kuchukua tahadhari kali ili kuepuka maradhi. Maonyo ya usafiri kutoka kwa vyanzo vya serikali yanapaswa kuchukuliwa kwa uzito.
translations.reference: Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

Travelers may also be exposed to several infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Areas where accommodation, sanitation, environmental hygiene, medical care, and water quality are of a high standard result in fewer health risks for travelers. Where these services are not available, travelers should take extreme precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from government sources should be taken seriously.

translations.source: Kinga: Kabla ya kusafiri: wasiliana na kituo cha dawa za usafiri au daktari, ikiwezekana wiki 4-8 kabla ya kuondoka ili kupokea chanjo zinazohitajika; soma kuhusu magonjwa ya eneo lako yanayohusiana na unakoenda; omba taarifa kuhusu hatari ya malaria na uzuiaji wa kuumwa na mbu. Hii inaweza kujumuisha kununua dawa zinazofaa za kuzuia na kufunga chandarua na dawa ya kufukuza wadudu; pata uchunguzi wa matibabu na meno;
translations.reference: Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

Vaccination: Before traveling: contact a travel clinic or doctor, ideally 4-8 weeks before departure to receive necessary vaccinations; read about diseases in your area related to where you are going; ask for information about the risk of malaria and mosquito bite prevention. This may include buying suitable preventative medication and using mosquito nets and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up.

translations.source: kupata maagizo ya dawa kulingana na urefu wa kukaa na kukusanya kit cha matibabu kinachofaa; nunua bima ya matibabu na bima inayofaa nje ya nchi Wakati wa kusafiri: kula tu chakula kilichopikwa vizuri na kunywa tu vinywaji baridi vya chupa au vifungashio. Chemsha maji ya kunywa ikiwa usalama ni wa shaka.
translations.reference: obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Obtain medical prescriptions according to the length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance and appropriate travel insurance. During travel: only eat well-cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold beverages. Boil drinking water if safety is questionable."

translations.source: kuwa na ufahamu wa ajali au matatizo yanayohusiana na trafiki, wanyama, mizio, jua na michezo; kubeba kadi inayoonyesha kundi lako la damu; kuvaa bangili ya tahadhari ya matibabu, ikiwa una mzio au ugonjwa sugu. Wasafiri wanapaswa kufanyiwa uchunguzi wa kimatibabu wanaporudi ikiwa: kurudi na homa kutoka nchi ambako malaria iko au inaweza kuwepo
translations.reference: be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, allergies, sun and sport; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet, if you have allergies or a chronic disease Travellers should obtain a medical examination on their return if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria is or may be present
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, insects, the sun, and sports; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet if you have allergies or chronic illnesses. Travelers should have a medical check-up upon returning if: returning with a fever from a country where malaria exists or may exist."

translations.source: kuteseka na ugonjwa sugu hupata ugonjwa kwa wiki zinazofuata baada ya kurudi nyumbani alipata matibabu ya malaria akiwa safarini inaweza kuwa wazi kwa ugonjwa mbaya wa kuambukiza alitumia zaidi ya miezi 3 katika nchi inayoendelea
translations.reference: suffer from a chronic disease experience illness in the weeks following their return home received treatment for malaria while travelling may have been exposed to a serious infectious disease spent more than 3 months in a developing country
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Suffering from a chronic illness, he contracted the disease in the weeks following his return home after receiving malaria treatment while traveling. He may be vulnerable to a serious infectious disease after spending more than 3 months in a developing country."

translations.source: Kifua kikuu Muhtasari: Kifua kikuu husababishwa na bakteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) na mara nyingi huathiri mapafu. Kifua kikuu huenezwa kwa njia ya hewa wakati watu wenye TB ya mapafu wanapokohoa, kupiga chafya au kutema mate. Mtu anahitaji kuvuta vijidudu vichache tu ili kuambukizwa. Kila mwaka, watu milioni 10 wanaugua kifua kikuu (TB).
translations.reference: Tuberculosis Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it most often affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with lung TB cough, sneeze or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people fall ill with tuberculosis (TB).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

**Tuberculosis Summary:** Tuberculosis is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and often affects the lungs. Tuberculosis is spread through the air when people with pulmonary TB cough, sneeze, or spit. A person only needs to inhale a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people get tuberculosis (TB).

translations.source: Licha ya kuwa ni ugonjwa unaozuilika na unaotibika, watu milioni 1.5 hufa kutokana na TB kila mwaka - na kuifanya kuwa muuaji mkuu wa kuambukiza duniani. Kifua kikuu ndicho kisababishi kikuu cha vifo vya watu walio na VVU na pia mchangiaji mkubwa katika ukinzani wa viua viini Watu wengi wanaougua TB wanaishi katika nchi zenye kipato cha chini na cha kati, lakini TB ipo duniani kote. Takriban nusu ya watu wote walio na TB wanaweza kupatikana katika nchi 8: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Ufilipino na Afrika Kusini. Takriban robo ya idadi ya watu duniani inakadiriwa kuwa wameambukizwa bakteria wa TB, lakini watu wengi hawataendelea kupata ugonjwa wa TB na wengine wataondoa maambukizi.
translations.reference: Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the world’s top infectious killer. TB is the leading cause of death of people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance Most of the people who fall ill with TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present all over the world. About half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines and South Africa. About a quarter of the global population is estimated to have been infected with TB bacteria, but most people will not go on to develop TB disease and some will clear the infection.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB every year – making it the world’s leading infectious killer. Tuberculosis is the primary cause of deaths in people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance. Most people who get TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present worldwide. Approximately half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, the Philippines, and South Africa. Roughly a quarter of the world’s population is estimated to be infected with TB bacteria, but most will not go on to develop TB and some will clear the infection.

translations.source: Wale ambao wameambukizwa lakini sio (bado) wagonjwa na ugonjwa huo hawawezi kuusambaza. Watu walioambukizwa na bakteria wa TB wana hatari ya maisha ya 5-10% ya kuugua TB. Wale walio na kinga dhaifu, kama vile watu wanaoishi na VVU, utapiamlo au kisukari, au watu wanaotumia tumbaku, wana hatari kubwa ya kuugua. Dalili: Dalili za kawaida za ugonjwa wa TB ni pamoja na: Kikohozi cha muda mrefu, maumivu ya kifua, udhaifu au uchovu, kupungua uzito, homa, jasho la usiku. Mara nyingi, dalili hizi zitakuwa nyepesi kwa miezi mingi, hivyo kusababisha kuchelewa kutafuta huduma na kuongeza hatari ya kueneza maambukizi kwa wengine.
translations.reference: Those who are infected but not (yet) ill with the disease cannot transmit it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5–10% lifetime risk of falling ill with TB. Those with compromised immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of falling ill. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: Prolonged cough, Chest pain, Weakness or fatigue, Weight loss, Fever, Night sweats Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, thus leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading the infection to others.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Those who are infected but not (yet) sick with the disease cannot spread it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5-10% lifetime risk of developing TB. Those with weakened immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition, or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of developing it. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: a persistent cough, chest pain, weakness or fatigue, weight loss, fever, night sweats. Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading infection to others."

translations.source: Mtoa huduma wa afya akishuku mgonjwa kuwa na ugonjwa wa TB, atampeleka mgonjwa kupima. Katika kesi ya ugonjwa unaoshukiwa wa TB ya mapafu, wagonjwa wataombwa kutoa sampuli ya makohozi kwa ajili ya kupima bakteria wa TB. Kwa ugonjwa wa TB usio wa mapafu, sampuli za maji na tishu zilizoathirika za mwili zinaweza kupimwa. WHO inapendekeza vipimo vya haraka vya uchunguzi wa molekuli kama vipimo vya awali kwa watu wanaoonyesha dalili na dalili za TB. Zana nyingine za uchunguzi zinaweza kujumuisha hadubini ya smear ya sputum na X-rays ya kifua.
translations.reference: If the healthcare provider suspects a patient to have TB disease, they will send the patient for testing. In the case of suspected lung TB disease, patients will be asked to give a sputum sample for testing for TB bacteria. For non-lung TB disease, samples of affected body fluids and tissue can be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools can include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"If a healthcare provider suspects a patient has TB, they will refer the patient for testing. In cases of suspected pulmonary TB, patients will be asked to provide a sputum sample for TB bacteria testing. For non-pulmonary TB, samples of fluids and affected body tissues may be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools may include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays."

translations.source: Kwa maambukizi ya TB, mtu huambukizwa na bakteria ya TB ambayo haifanyi kazi katika mwili. Maambukizi haya yanaweza kukua na kuwa ugonjwa wa TB ikiwa mfumo wao wa kinga utadhoofika. Watu wenye maambukizi ya TB hawaonyeshi dalili zozote za TB. Ili kutambua maambukizi ya TB, watoa huduma za afya watachunguza wagonjwa walio katika hatari ili kudhibiti TB hai, na wanaweza kutumia kipimo cha ngozi au damu ili kuangalia maambukizi ya TB. Matibabu: Ugonjwa wa TB unatibika.
translations.reference: With TB infection, a person gets infected with TB bacteria that lie inactive in the body. This infection can develop into TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infection do not show any signs or symptoms of TB. To identify TB infection, healthcare providers will screen at-risk patients to rule out active TB, and they may use a skin or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is curable.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

"For TB infections, a person is infected with TB bacteria that is inactive in the body. These infections can develop into active TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infections do not show any symptoms of TB. To detect TB infections, healthcare providers will screen patients at risk to control active TB, and they may use a skin test or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is treatable."

translations.source: Inatibiwa kwa kozi ya kawaida ya miezi 6 ya antibiotiki 4. Dawa za kawaida ni pamoja na rifampicin na isoniazid. Katika baadhi ya matukio, bakteria ya TB haijibu dawa za kawaida. Katika kesi hii, mgonjwa ana TB sugu ya dawa. kwa TB sugu ya dawa ni ya mda mrefu na ngumu zaidi.
translations.reference: It is treated by standard 6 month course of 4 antibiotics. Common drugs include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases the TB bacteria does not respond to the standard drugs. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"It is treated with a standard course of 6 months of 4 antibiotics. Common medications include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases, the TB bacteria does not respond to standard medications. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. Treatment for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex."

translations.source: Muda wa dawa za TB hutolewa kwa mgonjwa kwa taarifa, usimamizi na usaidizi na mfanyakazi wa afya au mtu wa kujitolea aliyefunzwa. Bila msaada kama huo, ufuasi wa matibabu unaweza kuwa mgumu. Ikiwa matibabu haijakamilika vizuri, ugonjwa unaweza kuwa sugu wa dawa na unaweza kuenea. Katika kesi ya maambukizi ya TB (ambapo mgonjwa ameambukizwa na bakteria ya TB lakini sio mgonjwa), matibabu ya kuzuia TB yanaweza kutolewa ili kukomesha kuanza kwa ugonjwa huo. Tiba hii hutumia dawa sawa kwa muda mfupi.
translations.reference: The course of TB drugs is provided to the patient with information, supervision and support by a health worker or trained volunteer. Without such support, treatment adherence can be difficult. If the treatment is not properly completed, the disease can become drug-resistant and can spread. In the case of TB infection (where the patient is infected with TB bacteria but not ill), TB preventive treatment can be given to stop the onset of disease. This treatment uses the same drugs for a shorter time.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"The duration of TB medication is provided to the patient with information, management, and support by a healthcare worker or a trained volunteer. Without such support, adherence to treatment can be difficult. If treatment is not completed properly, the disease can become drug-resistant and can spread. In the case of TB infection (where the patient has been infected with TB bacteria but is not sick), preventive TB treatment may be provided to prevent the onset of the disease. This treatment uses the same drugs for a shorter period."

translations.source: Chaguo za matibabu za hivi majuzi zimefupisha muda wa matibabu hadi mwezi 1 au 3 tu, ikilinganishwa na miezi 6 iliyopita.
translations.reference: Recent treatment options have shortened the duration to treatment to only 1 or 3 months, as compared to 6 months in the past.
translations.hypothesis: Recent medical treatments have shortened the treatment period to just 1 or 3 months, compared to the 6 months previously.

translations.source: Mionzi ya ultraviolet Muhtasari: Mionzi ya Ultraviolet (UV) hufunika masafa ya urefu wa 100-400 nm, ambayo ni masafa ya juu na urefu wa chini wa mawimbi kuliko mwanga unaoonekana. Mionzi ya UV hutoka kwa jua, lakini pia inaweza kuundwa na vyanzo vya bandia vinavyotumiwa katika sekta, biashara na burudani. Kanda ya UV inashughulikia safu ya urefu wa 100-400 nm na imegawanywa katika bendi tatu: UVA (315-400 nm) UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). Mwangaza wa jua unapopita kwenye angahewa, UVC zote na takriban 90% ya mionzi ya UVB humezwa na ozoni, mvuke wa maji, oksijeni na dioksidi kaboni.
translations.reference: Ultraviolet radiation Overview: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers the wavelength range of 100–400 nm, which is a higher frequency and lower wavelength than visible light. UV radiation comes naturally from the sun, but it can also be created by artificial sources used in industry, commerce and recreation. The UV region covers the wavelength range 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and approximately 90% of UVB radiation is absorbed by ozone, water vapour, oxygen and carbon dioxide.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Swahili text:

**Ultraviolet Radiation Summary:** Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers a range of wavelengths from 100-400 nm, which is a higher frequency and shorter wavelength range than visible light. UV radiation comes from the sun, but can also be produced by artificial sources used in industry, commerce, and recreation. The UV range covers a range of wavelengths from 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm), UVB (280-315 nm), UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and approximately 90% of UVB radiation are absorbed by ozone, water vapor, oxygen, and carbon dioxide.

translations.source: Mionzi ya UVA huathiriwi kidogo na angahewa. Kwa hiyo, mionzi ya UV inayofikia uso wa Dunia kwa kiasi kikubwa inajumuisha UVA na sehemu ndogo ya UVB. Kiasi cha mionzi ya UV kutoka kwenye jua ambayo hupiga uso wa Dunia inategemea mambo kadhaa, ikiwa ni pamoja na urefu wa jua angani, latitudo, kifuniko cha mawingu, urefu, unene wa safu ya ozoni na kutafakari kwa ardhi. Kupungua kwa tabaka la ozoni kwa sababu ya uchafuzi unaotengenezwa na binadamu huongeza kiwango cha UVA na UVB kinachofika kwenye uso. Hii inaweza kuathiri afya ya binadamu, wanyama, viumbe vya baharini na maisha ya mimea.
translations.reference: UVA radiation is less affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the UV radiation reaching the Earth’s surface is largely composed of UVA with a small UVB component. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that hits the Earth’s surface depends on several factors, including the sun’s height in the sky, latitude, cloud cover, altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer and ground reflection. Reductions in the ozone layer due to human-created pollution increase the amount of UVA and UVB that reaches the surface. This can impact human health, animals, marine organisms and plant life.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"UVA rays are minimally affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the majority of UV radiation reaching the Earth's surface consists of UVA and a small portion of UVB. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that hits the Earth's surface depends on several factors, including the sun's angle in the sky, latitude, cloud cover, altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer, and the reflection of the land. Depletion of the ozone layer due to human-made pollution increases the levels of UVA and UVB reaching the surface. This can affect the health of humans, animals, marine organisms, and plant life."

translations.source: Kwa wanadamu, kuongezeka kwa mfiduo wa UV kunaweza kusababisha saratani ya ngozi, mtoto wa jicho na uharibifu wa mfumo wa kinga. Kinga: Kuongezeka kwa idadi ya visa vya saratani ya ngozi katika miongo kadhaa iliyopita kunahusiana sana na kuongezeka kwa mwanga wa jua wakati wa shughuli za nje na vyanzo bandia vya mionzi ya UV kama vile taa za jua na vitanda vya ngozi. Mfiduo kupita kiasi pia ndio sababu kuu ya athari mbaya kwa macho na mfumo wa kinga. Kuchukua tahadhari chache rahisi kunaweza kupunguza sana hatari ya hali hizi za afya: Weka kikomo wakati wa jua la mchana kutoka 10 asubuhi hadi 4 p.m., hasa siku ambazo index ya UV iko juu. Wakati miale ya UV ni mikali zaidi na lazima uwe nje, jaribu kutafuta kivuli na kuvaa mavazi ya kujikinga, ikiwa ni pamoja na kofia yenye ukingo ambayo hutoa ulinzi wa jua kwa kichwa chako, uso na shingo.
translations.reference: In humans, increased UV exposure can cause skin cancers, cataracts and immune system damage. Prevention: The rise in the number of cases of skin cancer over the past decades is strongly related to increased exposure to the sun during outdoor activities and to artificial sources of UV radiation such as sunlamps and tanning beds. Overexposure is also the underlying cause of harmful effects on the eyes and immune system. Adopting a few simple precautions can greatly reduce the risk of these health conditions: Limit time in the midday sun from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., particularly on days when the UV index is high When UV rays are most intense and you must be outside, try to find shade and wear protective clothing, including a brimmed hat that provides sun protection for your head, face and neck.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

For humans, increased UV exposure can cause skin cancer, eye damage, and damage to the immune system. Protection: The increase in cases of skin cancer over the past few decades is strongly linked to increased sunlight during outdoor activities and artificial sources of UV radiation such as sun lamps and tanning beds. Excessive exposure is also the main cause of adverse effects on the eyes and immune system. Taking a few simple precautions can significantly reduce the risk of these health conditions: Limit time in the sun from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., especially on days when the UV index is high. When UV rays are strongest and you must be outside, try to seek shade and wear protective clothing, including a hat with a brim that provides sun protection for your head, face, and neck.

translations.source: Tumia kinga ya jua yenye wigo mpana wa angalau SPF 15 - bora zaidi - na upake tena kila saa 2. Epuka taa za jua na vyumba vya ngozi, kwani vinajulikana kuharibu ngozi na macho. Mionzi ya UV ya watoto inapaswa kupunguzwa kwa uangalifu kwa sababu wako katika hali ya ukuaji na kwa hivyo huathirika zaidi na matishio ya mazingira kuliko watu wazima. Kazi nyingi muhimu kama vile mfumo wa kinga haujatengenezwa kikamilifu wakati wa kuzaliwa, na mazingira yasiyo salama yanaweza kuingilia ukuaji wao wa kawaida. Majibu ya WHO: WHO inafanya kazi na Nchi Wanachama na washirika ili kuongeza uelewa wa umma juu ya athari za mionzi ya urujuanimno kupita kiasi.
translations.reference: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen of at least 15 SPF – ideally higher– and reapply every 2 hours. Avoid sunlamps and tanning parlours, as they are known to damage the skin and eyes. Children’s UV exposure should be carefully limited because they are in a dynamic state of growth and therefore more susceptible to environmental threats than adults. Many vital functions such as the immune system are not fully developed at birth, and unsafe environments may interfere with their normal development. WHO Response: WHO works with Member States and partners to increase public understanding of the effects of overexposure to ultraviolet radiation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

"Use sunscreen with a broad spectrum of at least SPF 15 – preferably higher – and reapply every 2 hours. Avoid tanning beds and sunlamps, as they are known to damage skin and eyes. Children's UV exposure should be minimized because they are in a growth phase and are therefore more susceptible to environmental hazards than adults. Many important functions like the immune system are not fully developed at birth, and unsafe environments can interfere with their normal development. WHO Response: WHO works with Member States and partners to increase public understanding of the effects of excessive ultraviolet radiation."

translations.source: Mpango wa INTERSUN ni ushirikiano kati ya WHO, Mpango wa Mazingira wa Umoja wa Mataifa, Shirika la Hali ya Hewa Duniani, Shirika la Kimataifa la Utafiti wa Saratani na Tume ya Kimataifa ya Ulinzi wa Mionzi isiyo ya Ion. Inakuza na kutathmini utafiti juu ya athari za kiafya za mionzi ya UV, na kukuza jibu linalofaa kupitia miongozo, mapendekezo na usambazaji wa habari. Malengo ya mpango huo ikiwa ni pamoja na kutoa ushauri wa kiutendaji na mzuri kuhusu athari za kiafya na kimazingira zitokanazo na mionzi ya jua, kuhimiza nchi kuchukua hatua ili kupunguza hatari za kiafya zitokanazo na UV na kutoa mwongozo kwa mamlaka za kitaifa kuhusu mipango ya uhamasishaji wa jua. Ili kufikia mwisho huu, mpango huo unashirikiana na wataalam na mashirika maalum kutekeleza shughuli muhimu za utafiti, kutambua na kuhesabu hatari za afya kutokana na mionzi ya UV, kuendeleza utabiri wa kuaminika wa matokeo ya afya na mazingira ya mabadiliko ya mfiduo wa UV na kupungua kwa ozoni ya stratospheric, na kuendeleza njia za vitendo za ufuatiliaji mabadiliko katika athari za kiafya zinazotokana na UV kwa wakati. Mpango huo hutoa zana na miongozo inayohusiana na fahirisi ya UV, ulinzi wa jua kwa watoto, vitanda vya jua bandia, utalii na afya ya kazini.
translations.reference: The INTERSUN Programme is a collaboration between WHO, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and evaluates research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops an appropriate response through guidelines, recommendations and information dissemination. The goals of the programme including providing practical and sound advice on the health and environmental impacts of UV exposure, to encourage countries to take action to reduce UV-induced health risks and to provide guidance to national authorities about sun awareness programmes. To this end, the programme collaborates with experts and specialist agencies to implement key research activities, identifies and quantifies health risks from UV radiation, develops reliable predictions of health and environmental consequences of changes in UV exposure with stratospheric ozone depletion, and develops practical ways of monitoring change in UV-induced health effects over time. The programme provides tools and guidelines related to the UV index, sun protection for children, artificial sunbeds, tourism and occupational health.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Swahili document:

The INTERSUN program is a collaboration between the World Health Organization, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency for Research on Cancer, and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and evaluates research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops an appropriate response through guidelines, recommendations, and the dissemination of information. The program's goals include providing practical and sound advice on the health and environmental effects resulting from solar radiation, encouraging countries to take action to reduce health risks arising from UV radiation, and providing guidance to national authorities on sun awareness programs.
